aliases: Vegetable Pulao / Veg Pulao / Sabzi Pulao

english: Vegetable Pulao / Veg Pulao / Sabzi Pulao

food_subgroup: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with mixed vegetables and aromatic spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Vegetable Pulao. Nearest parent is a mixed vegetable rice dish. A new concept for Indian pulao preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed vegetable aromatic rice dish (pulao/pilaf) (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Vegetable Pulao; A03SL used as closest parent for mixed vegetable rice preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Vegetable Pulao as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Vegetable Pulao is approximately 300 g, providing around 444 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN; component data for cooked basmati rice and mixed vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using component data for cooked basmati rice and mixed vegetables (peas, carrot, beans, potato) combined in proportions typical of restaurant-style Vegetable Pulao. No single exact entry for Vegetable Pulao exists in ICMR-NIN; values represent a weighted composite consistent with published Indian food composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — cooked basmati rice with vegetables; analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 56 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice (GI ~50–58). The addition of mixed vegetables and fibre moderates the overall GI slightly above plain basmati rice. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (56 × 27.5 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 46.2. This is a high GL per plate due to the large serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 168 mg per 100 g (504 mg per 300 g plate); partially offset by potassium 142 mg per 100 g (426 mg per plate)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should be aware that a standard 300 g plate provides approximately 504 mg sodium; pairing with a low-sodium accompaniment such as plain raita and limiting portion size to 200 g can help keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~56) but high glycemic load (~46) per standard 300 g plate due to large carbohydrate quantity; dietary fibre from mixed vegetables partially moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g to reduce glycemic load to a manageable range, pair with a high-fibre or protein-rich side such as dal or raita, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the substantial carbohydrate content of a full plate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.5 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, cholesterol 6 mg per 100 g, and antioxidant-rich whole spices (cinnamon, cardamom, cloves)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable Pulao is a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal added fat; its low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, and anti-inflammatory aromatic spices support cardiovascular health, though sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole aromatic spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves) with documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The whole spices used in Vegetable Pulao have documented anti-inflammatory properties and the mixed vegetables provide diverse antioxidants, making this dish a suitable option for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Pulao is a fragrant, colourful Indian rice dish in which basmati rice is cooked by the absorption method together with mixed vegetables — typically peas, carrot, green beans, and potato — and whole aromatic spices including bay leaf, cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves, along with fried onion. The result is fluffy, separate-grained rice that is mildly spiced and aromatic. Unlike biryani, which involves layering and a more complex spice paste, Vegetable Pulao is a simpler one-pot preparation where rice and vegetables cook together from the start. The dish is lighter than biryani and is served with raita or dal as accompaniments. It is a staple of North Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and is widely available in Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, a 300 g plate provides approximately 444 kcal, 9.6 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 82.5 g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index (~56) and dietary fibre from the vegetables make it a moderately suitable choice for individuals managing blood glucose, though the glycemic load per full plate is high (~46) and portion control is recommended for diabetics. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked basmati rice and mixed vegetables rather than a single exact Vegetable Pulao entry; the composite is consistent with published Indian food composition data but involves proportional estimation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked basmati rice.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this mixed vegetable rice preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the ICMR-NIN component data used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed consistent with ICMR-NIN data for vegetable rice preparations). Glycemic load per 300 g plate is approximately 46.2, which is high; portion control is recommended for individuals managing blood glucose. The food_subgroup field retains the descriptive phrase from the input record as it accurately characterises the dish.
